info.name: Orbital-6986 Integration Tests
info.description: API-level integration tests for Nutrinus: Firebase Auth -> Firestore -> Firestore Security Rules. Run against the 'Orbital Local' environment.

item.name: 1.1 Get Auth Token - Test User A

request.description: Self-healing: tries sign-up first; if the account already exists it automatically falls back to sign-in. Safe to click every single run - no need to remember which request to use.

exec: pm.test('Test User A: signed up and token captured', () => true);
pm.test('Test User A: signup failed as expected (already exists)', function () {
pm.test('Test User A: fallback sign-in succeeded', function () {

item.name: 1.2 Get Auth Token - Test User B

request.description: Second identity, used only to prove User A's data is NOT readable/writable by User B. Same self-healing sign-up/sign-in fallback as 1.1.

exec: pm.test('Test User B: signed up and token captured', () => true);
pm.test('Test User B: signup failed as expected (already exists)', function () {
pm.test('Test User B: fallback sign-in succeeded', function () {

item.name: 2.2 Read Back Own Profile (assert write persisted)

exec: pm.test('name field matches what was written', () => json.fields.name.stringValue === 'Postman Tester');

item.name: 2.3 Simulate Returning User (fresh sign-in, new session)

request.description: User Story: 'As a returning user, I want my profile and meal logs to be saved so I can continue tracking across sessions.' This gets a genuinely NEW token (simulating closing the app and coming back later), independent of whatever token 1.1/1.2 produced earlier in this run.

item.name: 2.4 Returning User Still Sees Previously Saved Profile

request.description: Proves persistence across sessions, not just read-after-write in the same run: uses the fresh token from 2.3, not the original token from folder 1, to confirm the profile saved in 2.1 is still there for a genuinely new sign-in.

exec: pm.test('name still matches after a fresh sign-in', () => json.fields.name.stringValue === 'Postman Tester');
pm.test('height still matches after a fresh sign-in', () => json.fields.height.stringValue === '170');
pm.test('weight still matches after a fresh sign-in', () => json.fields.weight.stringValue === '60');
pm.test('profileCreated still true after a fresh sign-in', () => json.fields.profileCreated.booleanValue === true);

item.name: 3 - Meal Log Integration (log food -> dashboard update)

request.description: Mirrors AppContext.tsx addFood(): setLoggedCounts(prev => ({...prev, [id]: (prev[id] || 0) + 1})), then persistLog's updateDoc(ref, { [`logs.${today}`]: loggedCounts }). Food id '1' = 'Chicken Rice' in data.ts. updateMask targets logs.postmanTestDate specifically (not the whole logs field) to match the app's real dot-path update, which only touches today's entry.

item.name: 3.2 Read Back After First Add (assert count == 1)

exec: pm.test('logged count is 1 after first add', () => count === '1');

request.description: Mirrors clicking '+ Add to Log' a second time on the same item: count goes from 1 to 2, same as addFood's (prev[id] || 0) + 1 branch.

item.name: 3.4 Read Back After Second Add (assert count == 2)

exec: pm.test('logged count is 2 after second add', () => count === '2');

request.description: Mirrors AppContext.tsx removeFood()'s 'else' branch: (next[id] || 0) <= 1 is false since count is 2, so it decrements to 1 instead of deleting the key.

item.name: 3.6 Read Back After Removing One (assert count == 1)

exec: pm.test('logged count is 1 after removing one', () => count === '1');

request.description: Mirrors removeFood()'s 'if' branch: (next[id] || 0) <= 1 is true since count is 1, so 'delete next[id]' removes the key entirely rather than decrementing to 0. The body sends an empty map for postmanTestDate to represent that deletion.

item.name: 3.8 Read Back After Full Removal (assert key deleted, not just 0)

exec: pm.test('food id 1 key is fully deleted, not present with value 0', () => !foodKeys.includes('1'));

request.description: No Authorization header at all. Rules require request.auth != null, so this must be denied.

item.name: 4.2 [Negative] Read User A's Profile Using User B's Token

request.description: Authenticated, but as a different user. Rules require request.auth.uid == userId, so User B must not be able to read User A's document.

exec: pm.test('Public read succeeds without auth (200)', () => pm.response.code === 200);

item.name: 5.2 [Negative] Write to foodItems (must be denied for everyone)

request.description: Rules say 'allow write: if false' for foodItems - even an authenticated user must be denied.

exec: pm.test('Write to foodItems is denied (403) even when authenticated', () => pm.response.code === 403);

request.description: Firebase Auth passwords are case-sensitive (hashed comparison). Flips the case of every letter in testPassword and confirms sign-in is rejected. Run after '1 - Auth' so Test User A already exists.
Flips the case of every letter in testEmail (matching the app's own lowercasing behavior in AppContext.tsx's getTrimmedEmail) and confirms sign-in still succeeds and resolves to the same uid.

exec: pm.test('Mixed-case email still signs in successfully (200)', function () {
pm.test('Resolves to the same uid regardless of email case', function () {

request.description: Identity Platform keys accounts by email regardless of which sign-in method created them (password vs Google), so this proves the same collision detection your app relies on in AppContext.tsx's 'auth/email-already-in-use' / 'auth/account-exists-with-different-credential' handling - a second signUp for an existing email is always rejected with EMAIL_EXISTS, whether the original account was password-based or Google-based.

item.name: 7 - Data Integrity & Boundary Edge Cases
item.description: Probes boundary/malformed inputs against the real Firestore/Auth API (still integration tests, just negative-path). 7.1-7.4 originally documented gaps in firestore.rules content validation; those rules were since added (isValidProfileWrite in firestore.rules), so 7.1-7.4 now prove the writes are correctly rejected. 7.5-7.9 remain documented, accepted gaps (log-count bounds, doc-existence, password strength). Run after '1 - Auth'. Run '8 - Cleanup' afterward.

item.name: 7.1 [Fixed] Garbage Profile Data Now Rejected (height=-500, weight=banana)

request.description: firestore.rules now runs isValidProfileWrite(), which requires height/weight to be numeric strings within sane bounds (mirrors data.ts's isValidHeight/isValidWeight). This was previously a documented gap (accepted with 200) - now closed at the database layer, independent of the client.

exec: pm.test('Firestore now rejects garbage profile data (403)', () => pm.response.code === 403);

item.name: 7.2 Read Back Confirms Rejected Write Had No Effect

request.description: Confirms the rejected write in 7.1 was atomic - the valid height/weight from 2.1 ('170'/'60') remain untouched, not partially overwritten.

exec: pm.test('height still holds the last valid value (170), not the rejected write', () => json.fields.height.stringValue === '170');
pm.test('weight still holds the last valid value (60), not the rejected write', () => json.fields.weight.stringValue === '60');

request.description: firestore.rules now requires data.profileCreated is bool. Writing it as the string 'false' - which would have silently type-confused the app's `if (data.profileCreated)` truthiness check - is now rejected at the database layer.

exec: pm.test('Firestore now rejects type-mismatched profileCreated (403)', () => pm.response.code === 403);

item.name: 7.4 Read Back Confirms profileCreated Still a Valid Boolean

exec: pm.test('profileCreated is still the real boolean true from 2.1, rejected write had no effect', () => {

request.description: addFood/removeFood only ever produce positive integers, but nothing on the server stops a direct write of a negative count. Correctness on the dashboard relies entirely on the client-side filter `(loggedCounts[f.id] || 0) > 0` in AppContext.tsx:324 - there is no server-side guarantee.

exec: pm.test('Firestore accepts a negative log count with no rejection (200)', () => pm.response.code === 200);

item.name: 7.6 Read Back Negative Log Count (confirms it was stored, not rejected)

exec: pm.test('negative count stored exactly as-is (-5), proving no server-side validation exists', () => count === '-5');

item.name: 7.7 Sign Up Fresh Ephemeral User (never had a profile)

request.description: Uses Postman's built-in {{$timestamp}} dynamic variable so the email is guaranteed unique on every single run - this account has genuinely never existed before, so it's guaranteed to have no Firestore document yet (saveProfile() has never been called for it).

item.name: 7.8 [Gap-Check] Read New User's Doc Before Any Profile Save (expect 404)

request.description: A user can exist in Firebase Auth without ever having a Firestore document (saveProfile() creates the doc, not sign-up). AppContext.tsx's onAuthStateChanged handler guards this with `if (userDoc.exists())` - this proves that guard is load-bearing, not dead code, by confirming the raw API really does 404 here.

exec: pm.test('Brand-new user doc does not exist yet (404)', () => pm.response.code === 404);

request.description: createAccountWithPassword in AppContext.tsx checks password.length < 6 CLIENT-SIDE before calling Firebase. This bypasses the app entirely and calls Firebase directly with a 3-character password, to confirm Firebase's own server-side validation is a real safety net, not just a UI nicety - unlike gaps 7.1-7.6, this one is expected to PASS in your favor.

exec: pm.test('Weak password is rejected server-side, independent of the app (400)', () => pm.response.code === 400);

request.description: Run manually at the end of a test session to remove test data. Do NOT include this in an automated Collection Runner pass if you want to re-run 2.2/3.2 afterwards.
